paragraphs: Muscle Functional Anatomy

Note: For the dynamic graphics to work properly in this section, this document should be viewed in Google Documents, which should happen automatically if you clicked on the link in the Facebook group. The graphics do not work when you save the document as a PDF or DOC(X) file.

Many of us learned functional anatomy from the outside-in: we see and do exercises and try to figure out which muscles they train based on what we see and feel. Here you’ll learn an inside-out approach. You’ll learn each major muscle’s anatomy and biomechanics. This knowledge will enable you to understand which muscles are trained by any movement. Functional anatomy is essential for effective exercise selection and injury management, so you’re expected to know all these muscle groups and their functions by heart.

If you’re interested in a complete overview of every muscle and all of its functions beyond what’s practically necessary for a fitness professional, the following resources are recommended. The kinesiology glossary will also come in handy to understand this module if you have no background in functional anatomy.
Joint articulations: an overview of the movements that occur in each joint and which muscles contribute to said movement.
Muscle directory: an overview of the muscles in the human body and their functions.
Kinesiology glossary: an overview of terminology relevant to this section.
Most of the graphics in this document are courtesy of the now discontinued Muscle Anatomy app, Gray’s Anatomy and Wikimedia Commons.
Muscle anatomy overview
This is where over 300 pound bodybuilders come in handy: visualizing all major muscle groups. These images will serve as a muscle location reference for each of the subsections to follow.
Now let’s look at each major muscle group individually and identify their biomechanical functions in the human body.
Pectoralis major (chest/‘pecs’)

The chest, formally the pectoralis major, is many men’s favorite muscle group. A mirror muscle that’s always in sight. To understand how to stimulate it properly, we first have to look at its attachments to the human skeleton. From this, we can deduce its biomechanical functions.
The fan-shaped pectoralis major has its origin on 2 bones:
The sternum
The clavicle
The muscle inserts on the humerus to fulfill its biomechanical functions. The pectoralis major has 2 parts: a clavicular (upper) part and a sternal (lower) head.
Biomechanical functions

The pecs have multiple biomechanical functions. In some of these functions, other muscles help along (synergists). Moreover, one head of the pecs may sometimes help more during any motion than the other head.
Entire pec muscle
Transverse (horizontal) adduction and transverse flexion 0 - 90°

Transverse means horizontal. Horizontal adduction and flexion are the primary functions of the pecs and constitute largely the same ‘fly’ or ‘hug’ movement. We call it flexion when the shoulder is internally rotated (thumbs pointing towards each other or down) and adduction when the shoulder is externally rotated (thumbs up). The image below shows transverse/horizontal adduction, because the thumbs are up.

The pecs have their highest internal moment arms for horizontal shoulder flexion - so when the shoulder is internally rotated - and leverage peaks around 45° of flexion after full extension when your elbows have decidedly come in front of your body. The pecs’ moment arms decrease in greater extension or flexion. In full extension, when your elbows are behind your body, the anterior deltoids have better leverage than the pecs.
Arm internal rotation 0 - 70°

Clavicular (upper) head of the pecs
Note: the images show the entire pec muscle firing, but only the upper/lower head should.
Shoulder flexion 0 - ~160°
Shoulder abduction 0 - ~180°
Pec activity during pure shoulder abduction without shoulder flexion, such as a behind the neck press, is low.
Sternal head of the pecs
Shoulder extension 170 - 0°
Shoulder adduction ~170 - 0°

Note how when the muscle fires in the graphics, it becomes orange. So in this case, activation of the pecs results in a downward pull on the arm. Only the lowest pec fibers are very active at higher angles of shoulder abduction, as most fibers have a poor moment arm for shoulder adduction above 120° of shoulder abduction.
Triceps brachii
The triceps encompasses the bulk of the upper arm and, when highly developed, creates the ‘horse shoe’ appearance of the back of the arm.
The triceps, as the name indicates (tri = 3, cep = head), has 3 heads.
The long head on the back of the arm originates from the scapula.
The lateral head on the outside of the arm originates from the top of the humerus.
The medial head is mostly covered by the other heads and originates from the middle of the humerus.
All 3 heads converge into a single tendon which inserts into the top part of the ulna on the olecranon and its attached fascia (lower forearm bone).
Biomechanical functions

The main function of the triceps brachii is elbow extension: straightening your arms. The triceps is the only muscle with this function. All three heads contribute to elbow extension (the image below only highlights the long head).
Elbow extension 145 - 0°

The triceps have the highest internal moment arm for elbow extension when your arms are nearly straight. Leverage deteriorates with increasing elbow flexion up to around a 20% loss when the arms are fully bent.

The triceps can operate effectively over the full range of elbow extension with relatively little loss of force production capacity when stretched, as it doesn’t change length much, at least when your arms are at your side in the case of the long head, which brings us to our next topic.
Long head
Shoulder extension 170 - 0° and hyperextension (when the elbow travels behind the torso) 0 - 40°.

Shoulder flexion increases the triceps’s moment arm for shoulder extension manyfold up to a peak around 90° when your arms are straight forward. The elbow flexion angle has little effect on the long head’s ability to produce force at the shoulder, as elbow flexion doesn’t change the length of the long head much. Even at its strongest position, the triceps is very weak compared to other shoulder extensors though, especially the lats.
Shoulder adduction (mainly when the shoulder is externally rotated; weak)

The long head of the triceps is more complex than the muscles you’ve learned about so far. It’s bi-articulate, meaning it crosses 2 joints, in this case the elbow and the shoulder. When a muscle contracts, it produces force at both joints. This can be problematic, as not all the muscle’s functions are always desirable at the same time. When a biarticulate muscle is flexed at both joints at the same time, it may enter active insufficiency: it becomes too shortened to effectively generate tension (muscle filaments can no longer slide) and can no longer assist in producing further movement, thereby limiting range of motion. In this position, a muscle cannot be trained effectively. To experience active insufficiency, extend your wrist (see image below). Now make a fist. No problem, right? Now flex your wrist and make a fist again. You should feel a cramping sensation in your hand or forearm. That’s active insufficiency of the flexor digitorum superficialis muscle in your forearms.

Similarly, when a biarticulate muscle is lengthened at both joints at the same time, it may enter passive insufficiency: the muscle is maximally lengthened, at which point it cannot produce further movement or produce significant force (actin and myosin cannot form effective cross-bridges anymore). While pop wisdom says excessively short muscles are a common cause of limited range of motion, passive insufficiency is actually quite rare and limited to biarticulate muscles, specifically cases where a high range of motion is required at two joints at the same time.

Due to its biarticulate role, the long head of the triceps enters active insufficiency - shortening so much that it cannot produce further tension and movement - when it has to maximally flex at the elbow and the shoulder at the same time (arm extended behind the body). So the long head of the triceps cannot effectively extend the elbow when the shoulder is fully extended or adducted.
Maximally stretching the triceps at the shoulder during shoulder flexion and at the elbow during elbow flexion (arm bent overhead) induces passive insufficiency.
We’ll go into more detail on the practical implications of bi-articulate muscles in the exercise selection module.
Latissimus dorsi (‘lats’)
The wings of the back. Huge lats earned Franco Columbu the nickname ‘the bat’.
The latissimus dorsi muscle inserts on the humerus, on the inside of the upper arm just in front of the tendon from the pecs, from a broad range of origins.

The spine from T7 all the way down to the sacrum, specifically the spinous processes of the lower 6 thoracic vertebrae (T7-T12), the supraspinous ligaments of all lumbar vertebrae, the thoracolumbar fascia attached to this part of the spine and the 5 fused sacral vertebrae (i.e. the sacrum).
The top of the pelvis, specifically the posterior iliac crest.
The lower 3 or 4 ribs.
The lower tip of the scapula (only some fibers, so the lats are very weak at the scapula).

The lats have a little helper on top of them called the teres major muscle. The teres major shares the functions of shoulder extension, adduction and internal rotation with the lats, so it’s often functionally indistinguishable and you don’t have to consider it during training program design. Any program that trains the lats well, will also train the teres major well.
Biomechanical functions
Shoulder extension 170 - 0°

The main function of the lats is to pull your elbows from the front of your body to anatomical position. The lats can achieve their highest muscle activation during shoulder extension [2], so this is arguably their primary function. Practically all lat training routines should have a full ROM shoulder extension exercise.

Peak shoulder extension internal moment arms occur at ~45° of shoulder flexion with poorer leverage in both higher and lower shoulder flexion [2, 3]. Beyond 120° of shoulder flexion, the lats have an internal moment arm of nearly zero. After this point, most shoulder extension is produced by the teres minor, teres major and rear deltoids.

Similarly, below 0°, many fibers of the lats are very weak and the other shoulder extensors take over most of the work. Below 0° of shoulder flexion means the elbows come beyond anatomical position, behind your body. If you pull your elbows back far enough, you’ll pull the muscle insertion point behind the origin, so the lats will even reverse function and become weak shoulder flexors.

The upper, thoracic fibers have the best leverage for shoulder extension. The body tends to preferentially recruit lat muscle fibers with better leverage, at least during submaximal contractions, so it’s plausible that shoulder extension exercises will slightly emphasize muscular development of the upper lat fibers, though shoulder extension still effectively trains the whole lats.

While the optimal length for force production is anatomical position like for most muscles, the lats experience a relatively small decrease in force production capacity at greater lengths: the lats are effective over a large range of motion.
Shoulder adduction ~170 - 0°

Shoulder adduction is the second most important function of the lats for the strength trainees, especially when the shoulder is somewhat externally rotated, such as during wide grip pull-ups (as opposed to supinated grip chin-ups).

The lats’ internal moment arm for adduction peaks around ~75° of shoulder abduction, when your elbows are above your shoulders, with a pronounced decrease in leverage when your elbows are lower or higher. Leverage remains positive over the entire range of motion, unlike during shoulder extension.

The lower, lumbopelvic muscle fibers have the best leverage for shoulder adduction and these seem to be preferentially recruited during shoulder adduction from a position of shoulder abduction. More advanced trainees will likely benefit from having a full ROM shoulder adduction exercise in addition to a shoulder extension exercise to maximize growth in all lat fibers.
Shoulder internal rotation 0 - 70°
Because of its insertion on the front part of the humerus, the lats also act as an internal arm rotator.
Horizontal shoulder extension (very weak due to a low internal moment arm)

Remember: it’s horizontal extension only when the shoulder is internally rotated. When the shoulder is externally rotated (thumbs pointing up), the lats cease to function as transverse shoulder extensors.
Scapular retraction (adduction) 0 - 25° (very weak)
The horizontal fibers of the lats attached to the scapula contribute most to this motion, as their line of pull suits this movement best. It is not a primary function of the lats as a whole.
Scapular depression 0 - 10° (very weak)
The part of the lats that originates on the iliac crest probably contributes most to this motion, looking at its line of pull. It is not a primary function of the lats as a whole.
Scapular downward rotation (very weak)

The scapula rotates down along with downward movement of the arm, such as during pull-ups. During overhead pressing, it rotates up. These are relatively complicated functions of the scapula that you don’t typically target with specific exercises.
Spinal extension, rotation and lateral flexion (very weak; see spinal erectors section)

Technically, the lats can also assist with lumbar spinal extension, rotation and lateral flexion, but only certain muscle fibers can achieve this and their leverage is so poor that the total force contribution is trivial. It’s unlikely spinal movements effectively stimulate the lats.
Biceps brachii / elbow flexors

The biceps has become a symbol of muscularity. The biceps are part of the elbow flexors and most exercise science studies will speak of the elbow flexors rather than the biceps. There are 2 more elbow flexors than the biceps.
The brachialis is a relatively small and simple muscle. It lies mostly underneath the biceps and is only visible in very lean or extraordinarily muscular individuals.

The brachioradialis is a larger and more visible muscle that crosses over from the upper arm (humerus) over the elbow joint into the forearm (radioulnar joint). Since it wraps around the arm from the outside to the inside, it can not only flex the elbow but also bring the forearm to neutral, despite being mono-articulate. When the forearm is pronated, like during pull-ups (palms facing away from you), it acts as a supinator like the biceps to bring the forearm back to neutral, but when the forearm is supinated, like during chin-ups, it acts as a pronator to again bring the forearm back to neutral. In neutral, like when using a hammer grip, the brachioradialis does not affect the forearm position and has optimal leverage for elbow flexion.

Since these 3 muscles are all elbow flexors, it’s impossible to isolate one over the others and any program that results in maximal biceps growth will generally maximize growth in the other elbow flexors as well. Since the biceps are the biggest and most visible of the elbow flexors, we often call all elbow flexion ‘biceps work’ and we can largely forget about the existence of the other elbow flexors for most intents and purposes.
As their name indicates, the biceps have 2 heads. Both biceps heads originate from the scapula.
The long, outer head originates from the scapula, specifically the supraglenoid tubercle.
The short, inner head originates from the coracoid process of the scapula, a bony hook in between the clavicle and the humerus.
Both heads join to form a tendon that inserts into the radius, the upper bone of the forearm, and nearby fascia.
Since the biceps span across the shoulder, the elbow and the radioulnar (forearm) joint, they’re a tri-articulate muscle.
Biomechanical functions
Elbow flexion 0 - 150°
Flexing the arms is the primary function of the biceps and other elbow flexors. All elbow flexors have optimum leverage for elbow flexion when the arm is bent 90°.

The biceps’s moment arm also increases with supination [2]: the more supinated the forearm, the better the biceps’s leverage for elbow flexion. The brachioradialis has the greatest elbow flexion moment arm when the forearm is in a neutral position (hammer grip).
The position of the shoulder has minimal effect on the elbow flexion moment arms of the elbow flexors.
Forearm supination 0 - 90°

The biceps has the best internal moment arm for supination when the forearm is pronated. Leverage deteriorates with increasing supination. Likewise, the brachioradialis is only an effective supinator when the forearms are pronated: it has no elbow supination moment arm at all past neutral.
Shoulder flexion 0 - 60° (very weak; partly indirect and passive by the tendon of the long head; first 30-60° only [2, 3])

The biceps can also help with shoulder abduction when the forearm is supinated, but it's again very weak in this function. The biceps mostly acts as a stabilizer at the shoulder rather than a prime mover. The role of the biceps at the shoulder is too weak to have any functional significance in terms of training programming when it comes to direct force production, but it’s still relevant because shoulder flexion shortens the length of the biceps, which weakens the biceps. Like most muscles, the elbow flexors can produce the most tension in anatomical position, which has the elbow flexors at close to their maximum muscle lengths [2]. All elbow flexors lose a great deal of force production capacity when they shorten. The brachialis loses about half of its strength; the short head of the biceps loses 80%; the long head of the biceps and the brachioradialis are barely active when fully shortened.
It should be no surprise then that the short head of the biceps reaches active insufficiency when fully shortened and passive insufficiency when fully stretched.
Trapezius (‘traps’)

The trapezius muscle has many different origins, owing to its diverse, fan-like orientation of muscle fibers. The fibers originate down from the skull to the twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12) and insert on the collarbone (clavicle) and shoulder blade (scapula). In contrast to most muscles, there is considerable controversy over the functions of the traps and many textbooks are not in line with direct research on the functional anatomy of the traps. The trapezius can be distinctly divided into 3 regions based on the origin and insertion of their muscle fibers.

The upper trapezius originates from 2 locations. The upper fibers originate relatively vertically from the back of the skull (occiput; specifically the external occipital protuberance and the medial third superior nuchal ligament). 
The lower fibers originate progressively more horizontally from the back of the cervical spine (C1 to C6, specifically the ligamentum nuchae ligaments attached to the vertebrae). 
All fibers insert on the collarbone (specifically the posterior border of the distal third of the clavicle).
The middle traps run nearly horizontally from the spinous processes of C7 to the acromion of the scapula (medial border) and from T1 to the top of the scapula (superior spine).
The lower traps fan out from the spinous processes of T2 to T12 and insert progressively downward on the deltoid tubercle of the scapula.
Biomechanical functions
Upper traps

The upper traps can be further separated into upper upper traps and lower upper traps. The upper fibers of the upper traps function at the neck, including the cervical spine, and the scapulae. The lower fibers of the upper traps can only move the shoulder blades.

Neck lateral flexion 
0 - 35° (upper fibers only)
Neck and cervical spine extension 65 - 0° and hyperextension 0-40° (upper fibers only)
Neck rotation 0 - ~30° on both sides (upper fibers only)
Scapular elevation 0 - 40° (indirect; the upper fibers of the middle traps might also contribute very weakly at low elevation)
Scapular retraction (weak; lower fibers only; indirectly only via pull on the clavicle)

The upper trapezius’s primary function is shoulder elevation. Upper trap muscle activity during neck movements, as measured by either EMG and MRI, is below the threshold commonly needed for maximal muscle growth.

The upper traps are traditionally targeted with barbell or dumbbell shrugs. However, in contrast to what many textbooks state, the upper traps cannot produce a direct scapular elevation moment (unlike the deeper levator scapulae muscle), as the upper traps insert on the clavicle, not the scapula. Moreover, the lower fibers run almost horizontally and thus cannot produce much vertical force. Rather than directly producing a scapular elevation moment, the upper traps pull medially on the clavicle, which in turn pulls up the scapula as the clavicle rotates inward and up. To engage as many upper trap muscle fibers as possible during scapular elevation, the shoulder needs to be somewhat abducted. The very limited research we have supports shoulder abduction improves upper trap muscle activity during shrugs.

Choi et al. (2015) found that performing shrugs in shoulder abduction angles of 90° (arms horizontal) or 150° (arms diagonally overhead) stimulated considerably greater upper trap muscle activity than performing them in 30° shoulder abduction, with the arms closer to the sides. The shrugs were done with unweighted, voluntary contractions, so the results may be confounded by total muscular demands, but these should be equal at 30° and 150° of shoulder abduction. It’s also possible that maximal contractions maximize muscle activity regardless of shoulder abduction angle, rendering these results irrelevant for strength training.

Pizzari et al. (2014) found that shrugs at 30° of shoulder abduction stimulated greater upper trap muscle activity than standard shrugs in 0° abduction. This study design was slightly better, as training intensity was low but at least normalized to 25% of maximal voluntary force output at 90° abduction. While this resulted in a 25% greater dumbbell weight in the standard shrug group, the researchers should have normalized the weight in each group relative to the force output in that position, so the results may still differ for strength training when both exercises are performed at the same training intensity (%1RM).

Castelein et al. (2016) found similar upper trap muscle activity between regular standard dumbbell shrugs and overhead shrugs. However, the standard dumbbell shrugs were performed with a 5 kg dumbbell, whereas the overhead shrugs were done without weight, making it questionable how the results would have been when equalized for training intensity. It’s plausible that full shoulder abduction is inferior to lesser shoulder abduction, because once the scapulae are fully upwardly rotated, the upper traps may again cease to become effective scapular elevators.

Further support for the relative inefficacy of standard shrug exercises for the upper traps comes from a study by Conley et al. (1997). They found that a full-body training program with conventional barbell exercises, including deadlifts, conventional shrugs and overhead presses, was incapable of stimulating significant upper trap or neck muscle growth over 12 weeks, whereas it did stimulate significant growth in the quads. A group performing the same program plus neck extensions achieved far greater growth in the neck muscles but not the upper traps, suggesting neck extension exercise is also not effective for the upper traps. That said, dumbbell shrugs have been found to achieve considerably greater muscle activity in the upper traps than dumbbell rows, lateral raises and shoulder rehab exercises, all performed at 85% of 1RM, so standard shrugs are almost certainly viable, just likely not optimal. Wide grip shrugs should be better.

Since the upper traps are a bi-articulate muscle, they can enter active insufficiency when fully contracted at both joints and passive insufficiency when fully stretched at both joints. In practice, this is generally only relevant in one scenario. When the neck is extended, the upper fibers of the upper traps can enter active insufficiency when trying to produce shoulder elevation movement. This is why you are stronger at shrugs when your head is kept down and this is the preferred way to execute them for trainees without neck injuries, and correspondingly the way almost all Strongman competitors train. It is not injurious to the spine or neck, because due to the traps’ solely indirect contribution to scapular elevation, most compressive force is born by the sternoclavicular joint, not the cervical spine.
Middle traps
Scapular retraction/adduction 0 - 25° (the lower traps and lower fibers of the upper traps contribute)
Pulling the shoulder blades back is the primary function of the middle traps. Scapular retraction is also called scapular external rotation.
Scapular upward rotation 0 - 60° (only after some rotation, which the serratus anterior typically initiates)
This is not a function you typically directly target with strength training. It occurs during overhead pressing.

The upper fibers of the middle traps may also contribute weakly to scapular elevation at low angles of elevation, while their insertion is still below their origin and the fibers still run at least slightly downward. However, you likely don’t have to count shoulder elevation exercises as effective training volume for the middle traps, because muscle activity of the middle traps during standard dumbbell shrugs is barely half of that you can achieve with horizontal pulling exercises at the same training intensity (85% of 1RM).
Lower traps
Scapular depression 0 - 10° (lower, diagonal fibers only)
Scapular retraction 0 - 25°
Scapular upward rotation 0 - 60°

The lower traps are commonly also depicted as scapular upward rotators, but their fiber orientation directly along the axis of rotation major direct moment during shoulder flexion. The lower traps likely function mostly as scapular stabilizers during upward rotation with shoulder flexion. In general, the traps function as stabilizers during many movements. During shoulder abduction, the lower traps can much more effectively assist the middle traps to upwardly rotate the shoulder blade, as illustrated below by Camargo & Neumann (2019).
The lower traps (LT) and the serratus anterior (SA) can upwardly rotate the shoulder blade.

The exact amount of force the traps can produce in any joint position have not been measured to our knowledge, but based on their sarcomere operating lengths, the trapezius seems to be at its optimum length to produce tension when close to its maximal length. It can likely produce the least force when fully contracted.

Note: We do not cover the highly complex scapular movements of anterior/posterior tilting and internal/external rotation. These movements are minimal and generally irrelevant for strength training program design.
Deltoids (shoulders/‘delts’)

The shoulder muscles - deltoids - have 3 heads: the front delts, the lateral delts, and the rear delts. Since the 3 heads surround the shoulder, the deltoids have functions that occur in a continuum with great regional differentiation. In fact, the most anterior fibers of the front delts and the most posterior fibers of the rear delts have directly opposing functions. The body will typically preferentially recruit the motor units with the best leverage to produce movement, at least during submaximal tasks.
The front/anterior delts have their origin on the clavicle.

The lateral/middle/side delts have their origin on the acromion. Note that the lateral delts are often erroneously called medial delts, but medial and lateral are opposite indications: medial refers to the median plane, the midline of the body, whereas lateral refers to the outsides of the body.
The rear/posterior delts have their origin on the spinal crest of the scapula.
All the heads insert on the lateral side of the humerus.
Biomechanical functions
Front deltoid
Transverse adduction and transverse flexion 0 - 90°

The front delts are very weak horizontal adductors (shoulder externally rotated; illustrated below) but powerful horizontal flexors (shoulder internally rotated). The front delts have their best leverage in full horizontal extension and until 45° of flexion, they have better leverage than the pecs. After that, when your elbows have come decidedly in front of your body, the pecs have considerably greater moment arms for horizontal shoulder flexion.
Shoulder flexion 0 - 160° (lateral delts also contribute, especially when the shoulder is internally rotated)

Raising your arms in front of you is the primary function of the anterior deltoids. They have much better leverage than the pecs, especially during overhead movements. The higher up your arms are, the better the delts’ leverage, at least up to 120° of flexion.
Shoulder internal rotation 0 - 70°
Lateral delts

Shoulder abduction 0 - 170° (front delts also contribute, especially when the shoulder is externally rotated, and the anterior fibers of the posterior delts might also contribute). The higher up your arms are, the better the delts’ leverage for shoulder abduction, at least up to 120° of flexion.
Rear delts
Transverse (= horizontal) abduction and extension 145 - 0° and posteriorly 0 - 45°

The lateral delts also contribute during horizontal abduction, i.e. when the shoulder is externally rotated. The rear delts have better leverage when the shoulder is internally rotated, i.e. during horizontal extension.
Shoulder extension 170 - 0° and hyperextension 0 - 40°

Unlike the other delts, the rear delts have their best leverage when your arms are at your sides or even behind your back. Their internal moment arms decrease with increased shoulder flexion. Since the pecs and lats are both incapable of extending the shoulder effectively beyond anatomical position, this makes the rear delts the primary hyperextensors of the shoulder: during pulling movements when the elbow comes behind your body, the rear delts contribute much of the force.
External rotation 0 - 90°

The exact amount of force each head of the delts can produce in any joint position has not been measured to our knowledge, but based on their sarcomere operating lengths, the front and side delts have a standard inverted U-shape length-tension relationship with optimum tension almost right at their middle length. The side delts are probably weakest in full contraction, more so than the front delts. The rear delts, interestingly, have an optimal length for force production near their maximal length and are likely weak when fully shortened.
Gluteals (butt/‘glutes’)
One of the largest muscles in the human body, the gluteus maximus is the primary muscle of interest to develop for many women.
The gluteus maximus has its origin on multiple locations:
The crest of the iliac part of the pelvis
The lateral margin of the coccyx
The dorsal surface of the sacrum

The muscle inserts into the lateral/posterior side of the femur (upper leg bone). However, the upper part of the muscle also connects to the posterior part of the iliotibial tract and the tensor fasciae latae (TFL) muscle.

The gluteus maximus is not the only gluteal muscle. It is accompanied by the gluteus minimus and medius (illustrated below). The gluteus minimus is not visible from the outside, so it is of no aesthetic concern. It functions primarily as a hip abductor. It can also assist with internal hip rotation during abduction (which is an uncommon movement pattern).

The gluteus medius is visible from the outside when well-developed. It is the muscle you see when you see someone’s glutes from the front, creating the appearance of wider hips. The glute medius’s functions are:

Hip abduction up to ~90° of hip flexion [2, 3, 4]. The gluteus medius is a strong pelvic stabilizer when standing upright, but it becomes a very weak abductor when you raise your legs (i.e. flex the hip).
Hip external rotation during parts of hip abduction.
Hip internal rotation (most anterior fibers only).

Since hip internal and external rotation are difficult to practically train, generally restricted to certain inconvenient band and cable exercises, that leaves hip abduction in a position of hip extension as the primary functions to exercise to stimulate the gluteus medius when all gluteus maximus functions are already trained. This can be achieved by standing or lying hip abduction exercises.
Biomechanical functions
Because of it lateral/posterior insertion on the femur, and its wide area of origin points, the gluteus maximus fulfill a variety of biomechanical functions.
Hip extension 0 - 120° (~20° past anatomical position; illustrated below)

The primary function of the gluteus maximus is hip extension, a function it shares with the hamstrings. When the knee is bent, the hamstrings are shortened and consequently weaker, so the gluteus maximus becomes the primary hip extensor. Bent-knee hip extension exercises are thus the most effective way to isolate the glutes, although it is not necessary to isolate the glutes to fully activate them.
The lower fibers of the glutes are stronger hip extensors than the upper fibers due to their leverage.

The glutes have the best leverage for hip extension — the highest internal moment arms — near full hip extension, in anatomical position. Based on the scant research we have on their length-tension relation, the glutes likely also produce the most force in this position with a decrease in further hip flexion.
Hip external rotation 0 - 45° (likely primarily upper fibers)

(Transverse) hip abduction 0 - 50° mainly transverse, i.e. when the hip is flexed like in the bottom position of a squat, and mainly the upper glute fibers. The gluteus medius and minimus are the primary hip abductors when you are not bending at the hip. The gluteus maximus gradually takes over the hip abduction role of the glute medius as you flex the hip.

Hip adduction? (lower fibers only) 0 - 30°: This is speculative, as anatomically it would seem like the lower fibers have a moment arm for hip adduction, but electromyography (EMG) research is unable to detect muscle activity during hip adduction.
Quadriceps (‘quads’)

The quadriceps comprises the front part of the thigh. Although it is named for having 4 heads – the rectus femoris and the 3 vasti – recent research has found there might actually be 5 distinct heads, adding the new tensor vastus intermedius (TVI, but contested here). The 4 vasti all have nearly identical functions, however, and all only cross the knee joint, so in practical terms only a distinction between the bi-articular (crossing 2 joints) rectus femoris and the vasti needs to be made.
The heads originate in the following locations:
Rectus femoris: the anterior inferior iliac spine and the superior part of the acetablulum
Vastus lateralis: the greater trochanter and the lateral side of the femur
Vastus intermedius/tensor vastus intermedius: the anterior and lateral side of femur
Vastus medialis: the medial side of the femur
All of the heads come together in the patellar tendon, which inserts into the top of the tibia (lower leg bone).
Biomechanical functions
Knee extension 150 - 0°
Straightening the knee is the primary function of all the heads of the quads. The quads are the only knee extensors in the human body.

The moment arms of the quads peak around 45° of knee flexion [2]. Leverage is still close to optimal up to full knee extension when your legs are straight. Bending the legs more than 45° significantly deteriorates their leverage.
Like most muscles, the quads can produce the most tension in anatomical position. The more you bend your leg, the less force your quads can internally produce.
Hip flexion 0 - 140° (rectus femoris only; ‘pulling the knee up’)
The rectus femoris has the best leverage for hip flexion around anatomical position. The more you pull up your knees, the worse its leverage.
The optimum length for muscle tension is also in anatomical position with a gradual decrease both when the muscle shortens and when it contracts.

The upper (proximal) part of the rectus femoris, closer to the hip, is most strongly active during hip flexion, whereas the lower (distal) part closer to the knee is preferentially recruited during knee extension.

Since the rectus femoris crosses both the knee and hip joints, it enters active insufficiency when trying to both fully extend the knee and flex the hip at the same time. The active insufficiency is so pronounced in this case that you can easily witness it yourself. Stand up and bring up your knee as high as you can. Now straighten your leg. You’ll see you cannot straighten it without lowering your knee. This is why you should lean back in your seat during leg extensions to stimulate the rectus femoris well.
Hamstrings (‘hams’)

Combined with the glutes, the hamstrings are the powerhouse of the human body. The hamstrings have 4 heads. The biceps femoris has a short head (mono-articular) and a long head (bi-articular). The other 2 heads are the semitendinosus and the semimembranosus.
Their origins are the following:
Biceps femoris (short head): the lateral/posterior side of the femur
Inserts into lateral side head of fibula
Biceps femoris (long head): the ischial tuberosity
Inserts into lateral side head of fibula
Semitendinosus: the ischial tuberosity
Inserts into medial side of tibia
Semimembranosus: the outer surface of the ischial tuberosity
Inserts into medial side of tibial head

All the hamstrings’ heads, aside from the biceps femoris (short head), originate in the ischial tuberosity of the pelvis. However, their insertion points vary, which allow this muscle group to serve a variety of biomechanical functions. The 2 primary functions of interest for strength trainees are knee flexion (bending the knee) and hip extension (thrusting the hips forward).
The biceps femoris (long head), semitendinosus, and semimembranosus all originate on the ischial tuberosity of the pelvis.
Biomechanical functions
Hip extension 0 - 120° (all heads except the short head of the biceps femoris)

The hamstrings have the best leverage for hip extension around 45° of hip flexion [2]. The hip extension moment arm decreases considerably in both higher and lower hip flexion. The hamstrings are relatively weak but still active during hip hyperextension.
Knee flexion 0 - 150° (all heads)
The other main biomechanical function of all heads of the hamstrings is knee flexion, i.e. bending the knee.
The hamstrings’ leverage for knee flexion is relatively constant until about 90°, after which bending the knee further greatly decreases the internal moment arm [2].

All heads of the hamstrings can produce the most tension in anatomical position with gradual loss of force production capacity at greater and smaller lengths. All heads of the hamstrings, interestingly except the semitendinosus but including the short head of the biceps femoris, are practically incapable of producing force when fully stretched or contracted. Consequently, the hamstrings, specifically the semimembranosus and the long head of the biceps femoris, enter passive insufficiency when stretched to near-maximal levels at both joints (straight legs and hips flexed). When fully contracting at both joints (knees bent and hips extended), the hamstrings will enter active insufficiency. Thus, it’s generally best not to perform leg curl exercises in full hip extension or hip extension exercises with bent knees to train the hamstrings. You cannot impose much active tension on the hamstrings in these positions.
The hamstrings can also externally and internally rotate the knee, but these functions generally have no practical application in training program design.
Knee internal rotation 0 - 10° (semitendinosus and semimembranosus only)
The medial, inner side of the hamstrings, the semimembranosus and semitendinosus, can rotate our feet inward. If you internally rotate both knees, your feet will point toward each other a bit.
Knee external rotation 0 - 30° (biceps femoris short and long head only)
The lateral, outer side of the hamstrings, the biceps femoris heads, can rotate our knees outward. If you externally rotate both knees, your feet will point away from each other a bit.
Calf muscles (‘calves’)

The calf muscles comprise of 2 different muscles: the gastrocnemius with a medial and lateral head and the soleus. The soleus lies in part beneath the gastrocnemii. The soleus is mono-articular and only acts at the ankle, whereas the gastrocnemii are bi-articular and also act at the knee.
The origins are the following:
Medial gastrocnemius: the medial side of the femur head
Lateral gastrocnemius: the lateral side of the femur head
Soleus: the posterior side of the upper fibula and the soleal line of the tibia
All three muscles fuse into the achilles tendon, which inserts into the heel of the foot.
Biomechanical functions
Ankle plantar flexion 0 - 50°
The main biomechanical function of the 2 calf muscles is plantar flexion: tipping up from the balls of your feet.
Knee flexion 0 - 150°: gastrocs only
Because the gastrocnemii cross the ankles as well as the knee, they contribute to knee flexion and enter active insufficiency when flexed to a certain level at both joints at the same time.

During leg curls, this means plantar-flexing your feet (straightening your ankles) will shorten the gastrocnemii, which will reduce their contribution to the movement. Unless you don’t want your calves to get any bigger, this is undesirable and you generally want to keep your toes curled up towards your knees (dorsiflexed) during leg curl movements so the gastrocs can contribute.

During calf raises, bending the knee shortens the gastrocs and can result in active insufficiency, which majorly reduces their force output. Subsequently, the soleus has to work harder to fulfill plantar flexion of the foot when the knees are bent, like during seated calf raises. Based on the difference in muscular tension, bent-knee calf raise movements effectively isolate the soleus.

Tension in the gastrocnemius heads also seems to be affected by foot position, though it’s unclear why this occurs biomechanically. Nunes et al. (2020) found that foot position during straight-leg calf raises in a leg press, as part of a full-body training program, affected which heads of the gastrocs grew more. Pointing the toes out resulted in more growth in the medial head, whereas pointing the toes in resulted in more growth in the lateral head, as you can see in the image below. These effects are in line with changes in muscle activity recorded during some electromyographic (EMG) research [2], though not all research found an effect of foot position on gastrocnemius muscle head specific muscle activity.
Spinal erectors

The spinal erectors, or erector spinae, are the collective name for a group of longer muscles on the back responsible for stabilization of the vertebral column. When well-developed, they create the 2 cobra muscles lying next to the spine, and they give bodybuilders in contest shape the ‘Christmas tree’ lower back. These muscles prevent your spine from folding over  during deadlifts and squats and perform several more biomechanical functions that aren’t as well known.

The 3 major spinal erector muscle groups are the longissimus, the iliocostalis and the spinalis, all of which are divided into multiple muscles along the spine: see the figures below. The spinalis muscles lie closest to the spine (most medial). At their outsides lie the longissimus muscles, which are the biggest of the erector spinae. At their outsides, furthest away from the spine (most lateral), lie the iliocostalis muscles.
Their origins and insertions are the following:
Longissimus: arises from the transverse processes of T1-T12 and the ribs of T5-T12 (thoracis), the transverse processes of C2-C6 (cervicis) and the mastoid process (capitis).
Inserts into the L1-L5 spinous processes, the sacrum, the ilium (thoracis), the transverse processes of T1-T5 (cervicis) and the transverse processes of C4-T4 (capitis)

Iliocostalis: arises from the first 4 lumbar transverse processes, the middle layer of thoracolumbar fascia and the lower 9 ribs (lumborum), transverse process of C7 and the upper 6 ribs (thoracis) and the posterior tubercles of C4-C6 (cervicis).
Inserts into: iliac crest (lumborum), the lower six ribs (thoracis), 3rd to 6th ribs (cervicis).
Spinalis: arises from the spinous processes T1-T4 (thoracis), C2-C4 (cervicis; not always present) and the back of the skull (capitis).
Inserts into: spinous processes of T11-T12 and L1-L2 (thoracis), variable connective tissues along C1-C7 and T1-T2 (cervicis), spines of C7 and T1 (capitis).
The anatomy of the human spine. Source
Biomechanical functions

Spinal extension 85 - 0° and hyperextension 0 - 25° (‘arching up the back’)
All 3 heads of the erector spinae contribute to spinal extension of all 3 parts of the spine (lumbar/lower, thoracic/upper and cervical/neck). Spinal extension is generally the only function of the erector spinae of concern to bodybuilders, since training the other functions is impractical and has minimal aesthetic benefit.
Spinal extension (left) and hyperextension (right)
Spinal lateral flexion 0 - 45°

The most outward spinal erector, the iliocostalis, can bend all 3 parts of the spine sideways. The more inner longissimus can only bend the upper cervical spine, our neck, sideways. The most inner spinalis can only laterally flex the cervical spine.
Spinal rotation 0 - 80° (very weak; iliocostalis only for the lumbar and thoracic spine; rotation of the neck/cervical spine is achieved by the longissimus and spinalis)
Abdominals (‘abs’)

The abs. The muscle group that stabilizes and bends the torso forward. Of these muscles, the rectus abdominis is the most well-known, because it’s responsible for ‘the sixpack’ many covet. The lateral parts of the abs are the external obliques. The inner parts of the abs are the internal obliques and the transversus abdominis: these are not of aesthetic concern, since they are not externally visible.

Many people think they need to train their abs to get a slim midsection. This is the spot reduction myth: exercising a body part does not result in more long term fat loss in that area than in other body parts [2, 3, 4].

In fact, abdominal training will thicken your waist. Muscle hypertrophy by definition increases, not decreases, muscle size. Excessive abdominal training can result in ‘ninja turtle physique’ and some modern physique competitors are actively avoiding abdominal training, sometimes even squats and deadlifts, to retain a slim waist. Having a visible sixpack is primarily about getting rid of the pudge covering your abs. ‘Abs are made in the kitchen’ is a cliché, because it’s true. In other words, all the crunches in the world won’t give you a sixpack if you’re not lean enough.

That said, one study found that it’s possible to actually induce some spot reduction by performing high intensity exercise for a body part and then following it up with cardio. Exercising a body part increases the rate of local fat mobilization. Following up with cardio may prevent re-esterification of the fatty acids, causing a shift in body fat distribution. So if spot reduction is possible, cardio is likely required directly post-workout. This induces an interference effect (see the cardio course module), leaving only one scenario where spot reduction may be useful in practice. Men’s Physique competitors that perform cardio may want to perform heavy ab training before their cardio sessions. The energy deficit and interference effect should prevent excessive waist thickening. In other physique classes, getting visible abs is not usually a problem. A shift in fat from the abs to the posterior chain may actually be detrimental to achieve pro level male bodybuilding conditioning by preventing visible glute striations.

Generally speaking, heavy compound exercises, including pull-up variations and anterior load carriage like overhead pressing and (front) squatting, are sufficient as ‘core training’ for bodybuilders, depending on the exact exercise selection and individual preferences. Only certain individuals with a particularly unpronounced rectus abdominis muscle even at low body fat percentages - generally people with very poor muscular potential - benefit aesthetically from abdominal isolation work.
The rectus abdominis originates on the pubis
Inserts into the xiphoid process of the sternum
The external obliques originate on the 5th to 12th ribs
Inserts into the anterior iliac crest and pubic crest and tubercle
Biomechanical functions
(Lumbar) spinal flexion (including posterior pelvic tilting) 0 - 85°
Both the rectus abdominis and external obliques have the biomechanical function of spinal flexion. For the rectus abdominis ‘sixpack’ muscle, it is their sole function.
Spinal lateral flexion 0 - 40° (obliques only)
Spinal rotation 0 - 40° (obliques only)

Intra-abdominal pressure (IAP)
Many core muscles, primarily the external obliques and to a lesser extent the rectus abdominis, contribute to intra-abdominal pressure (IAP). IAP can stabilize the spine during heavy lifting. It is why you often intuitively hold your breath when you approach muscular failure during a strength exercise, especially during exercises with spinal loading like squats and deadlifts. More deliberate use of this function is achieved by the Valsalva maneuver: breathing in deeply through your abdomen before a repetition and holding the air in to increase pressure in the abdominal area.
The forearms

The forearms are anatomically very complex compared to all the bigger muscles you’ve learned about so far. Just have a look at the images below from the Encyclopedia Brittanica of the forearms’ muscular anatomy as shown from the outside (top) and the inside (bottom; think of looking at someone’s forearms in the bottom position of a barbell biceps curl). Not only are there many muscles, many of them are bi-articulate and some even tri-articulate. It’s not realistic to know all of these muscles and their functions by heart.

Given the anatomical complexity of the forearms, fully optimizing their training requires a lot of different exercises that require loaded wrist movements in all wrist directions. These exercises tend to be impractical to do without special equipment. Moreover, you will not see much in return for your efforts, as the forearm muscles are small and many of them are covered by each other, so they are not easily visible from the outside. Plus, conventional strength training programs without forearm isolation exercises already stimulate significant development of the forearms, in particular via arm training. Many forearm muscles are weak elbow flexors or extensors, so they will be trained to some degree along with most biceps and triceps exercises. The act of gripping heavy weights, in particular during deadlifts or shrugs but also during bench pressing, chin-ups, etc. also stimulates significant forearm muscle recruitment. Dedicated forearm isolation training generally does not improve your strength during other exercises, such as squats or bench presses. Given the high effort involved for limited results, and often some wrist injuries along the way, most people don’t bother with forearm training.

However, if you’re prepared to do a significant amount of extra work, adding forearm training to strength training programs without forearm isolation work does improve forearm strength and size. Despite the large number of forearm muscles, we can separate them quite neatly into 2 functional categories and treat those as if they were 2 muscles: the wrist extensors and the wrist flexors. An economical way to add forearm training to any program is to pretend like those muscle groups are 2 separate muscle groups and to train them with wrist extension and wrist flexion exercises with half the volume you do for your biceps. The forearms will generally already be stimulated by other exercises to a significant degree, so training them with the same volume as you train other muscles is generally excessive.
The neck
Much like the forearms, the anatomy of the neck muscles is very complex, as you can see in the images below. You’re not expected to know all these muscles and their functions by heart.

These muscles together perform the following main neck movements: bending the neck to the side (lateral flexion), bending the neck to the front (flexion) or back (extension) and looking to the side (rotation), as illustrated below.

Since these movements are not commonly trained during conventional strength training exercises, the neck muscles do not receive appreciable stimulus for muscle growth from conventional strength training. Even shrugs, deadlifts and rows do not induce significant muscle growth in the neck muscles, whereas neck exercises do. Thus, training the neck muscles requires special exercises. These exercises generally require special equipment, such as a neck harness (illustrated below). You can put a plate on your skull to do some of the exercises, but it’s impractical, often painful, not very safe and not feasible with heavy loads. Sore neck muscles can also cause headaches and neck injuries tend to be very painful and annoying in daily life. As a result, even people that would like a thicker neck — not everyone finds it aesthetic — often don’t bother with neck training. Neck training is typically reserved for martial artists and race car drivers that do it primarily for functional reasons.
Neck extensions with a neck harness.
If you’re willing to go the extra mile to train your neck, you need to understand which muscles perform which functions. This is summarized in the following chart from Ackland et al. (2011).

The functional anatomy of the neck muscles is terribly complex, but just like with the forearms, we can simplify neck training considerably by dividing the neck into 2 main functional groups: neck flexors and neck extensors. The most important muscle to train for a wider neck is the sternocleidomastoid (SCM), as it’s relatively big and it spans across the sides of the neck. The other neck muscles are far less visible from the outside. The sternocleidomastoid is the primary neck flexor, so neck flexion is arguably the type of neck movement with the biggest visual effectiveness. If you then add neck extension, together those movements train all the neck muscles to at least some degree, except for the scalene muscles, which are purely involved in lateral flexion. These are small, deep muscles anyway, so they’re of no considerable aesthetic concern.

In conclusion, a simple programming strategy for those that want to train their neck is to treat the neck as 2 muscle groups: flexors and extensors. These 2 functions also happen to be the easiest to train. Even a plate on your skull is doable for high rep work neck flexion and extension while lying prone or supine on a bench with your head off the bench. A neck harness makes neck training much more comfortable though.

Those muscles can in principle be treated like any other muscle group in terms of training volume, intensity, etc. However, it’s advisable to err on the lower side with training volume and especially training intensity to prevent nasty neck injuries. It’s prudent to start with a single set per exercise at 20-30 RM and gradually work up to the optimal training volumes, increasing the training intensity only as needed to gain further strength. Note that if you’ve never done neck training, your neck muscles are essentially untrained, even if you’re an otherwise advanced trainee, so you should train them as such.
Exam

For the exam and as a PT you should know all major muscles’ functions by heart. You should be able to understand texts referring to terms like scapular elevation and be able to correctly name the movements that take place during any exercise in the gym.
